original_reasoning: The Arabic root centers on filling watercourses, and the Greek gloss is another river name.
Arabic settling or sinking readily extends to quieting and becoming still; Greek means 'be quiet, rest'.
Arabic explicitly mentions Cyprus and excellent copper, while Greek means 'weighed down with bronze'; both meet clearly in the copper/bronze semantic field.

Arabic البينيث: a type of sea fish (named with ب-ي-ن-ث). Greek βένθος: depth (of sea). A deep-sea fish and the depth of the sea — clear semantic field match. Arabic borrowed the Greek term for the deep to name this fish.

Arabic التغران: boiling/effervescence (also as proper name). Greek Τιγράνης: Tigranes (Armenian royal name). The Arabic التغران as a proper name phonologically matches Tigranes, and both likely share an Iranian/Semitic root. Strong phonological convergence.

Arabic التنتل: short/small person. Greek Τάνταλος: Tantalus (king who suffered eternal hunger/thirst). Tantalus's punishment of being just-out-of-reach connects to the Arabic 'one who is diminished/lacking.' Phonological match near-perfect; mythological borrowing plausible.
Arabic الشرشق = a loose/promiscuous woman, Greek κασωρίς = strumpet/prostitute — direct semantic match
Arabic: fire/burning. Greek: fire-related — strong semantic match.
